content: Treatment protocols are determined by cancer stage. Stage 0 (in situ): local excision or topical therapy, cure rates >95%. Stage I: surgery alone often curative; adjuvant therapy considered based on risk factors (e.g., breast cancer Stage I may receive radiation post-lumpectomy). Stage II: multimodal therapy - surgery plus adjuvant chemotherapy/radiation; neoadjuvant therapy increasingly used to downstage tumors. Stage III: aggressive multimodal treatment - neoadjuvant chemotherapy/radiation followed by surgery, or definitive chemoradiation; adjuvant therapy standard. Stage IV: systemic therapy primary (chemotherapy, targeted therapy, immunotherapy); palliative radiation/surgery for symptom control; oligometastatic disease may benefit from metastasectomy. Stage-directed trials show 5-year survival varies dramatically: Stage I breast cancer >99%, Stage IV <30%; Stage I colon cancer >90%, Stage IV <15%. Restaging after neoadjuvant therapy guides subsequent treatment.
